content.full_text:
be used to determine original notice

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance/Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322.5660                    322.5901              322.6441              322.6444

December 5, 1977

Ms. Betty Jo Smith
Staff Counsel
Lieutenant Governor's Office
State Capitol
Sacramento, California  95814

Dear Ms. Smith:

     This letter is in response to your request for an informal
opinion concerning the applicability of the Political Reform
Act to the staff of the Border Area Development Study. The
facts that we have been provided are as follows:

     The Office of the Lieutenant Governor received funding
from the U.S. Department of Commerce, Economic Development
Administration (EDA) to complete the Border Area Development
Study (Study). Funding for the Study is provided in part by
EDA, and the remainder with in-kind contributions from the
State of California through the Lieutenant Governor's Office.

     The Study will look at the economic conditions of the
counties in California that border Mexico, and analyze how
they are affected economically by their neighboring counties
across the border in Mexico. When completed the study will
be forwarded to the Southwest Regional Border States Commission.1/ This Commission consists of the governors of the
four states that border Mexico (California, Arizona, New
Mexico, and Texas), and a presidential appointee. The
Commission's objective is to determine ways to improve
the economic conditions of the thirty-six counties that
border Mexico, and to forward recommendations to the
President.


_______________________

1/ The study is scheduled for completion in December
of this year.

Betty Jo Smith
December 5, 1977

Page Two

Currently, there is a full-time staff of five individuals
working on the Study. This staff is made up of a director,
assistant director, two researchers, and a secretary. 2/ In
addition, the staff has two employees under contract as con-
sultants. The consultants, formerly classified as college
interns in the Lieutenant Governor's Office, operate under
the direction and control of J. Barry Fadem (formerly of the
Lieutenant Governor's Office), and Bob Holmes, the current
director of the Study. The consultants are under contract
to do research assessing the economic impact of illegal
aliens in the border counties area. They have no authority
to commit the Study to a course of action, nor are any of
their conclusions or decisions binding on the Study.

All the funds for this study were placed in a special
account by the Department of Finance, after the Legislature
officially approved the program calling for the Study. The
funds are administered by Richard Howe, the Study's fiscal
officer. Mr. Howe works in the Lieutenant Governor's Office.

The Study is funded substantially by EDA; part of the
funds they provide are used to pay the salary of the staff
(including the consultants). However, the staff is paid
through the special account established by the state. The
checks are drawn on a state account, and the staff receives
all the benefits that normally accrue to state employees
working in the Lieutenant Governor's Office.

The questions presented are:

(1) Whether the above-described staff of the Study
are federal or state employees, and

(2) Given that the Study is a federally and state
funded project administered by a state office, what pro-
visions of the Political Reform Act are applicable to
the Study's staff?

2/ The director is appointed by the Lieutenant Governor
and is subject to dismissal by him. The rest of the staff is
hired by the director of the Study. The director's appoint-
ments are subject to the approval of the Lieutenant Governor
and EDA. EDA approval is perfunctory. Their main concern is
to make sure the program is not being padded with blatantly
unqualified individuals.

Betty Jo Smith
December 5, 1977                                                    Page Three


                                    I.

        It is our opinion that the staff (including the consul-
tants) of this study are state employees. Although the Study
receives a substantial amount of federal money, part of which
pays salaries, the structure of the program suggests that the
staff shall be considered state employees. The program was
formulated in the Lieutenant Governor's Office, and the
request for federal funds was initiated by that office as
well. The funds are administered by the Lieutenant Governor's
Office and by no one else, including the Study's staff. As
stated previously, the checks used to pay the staff are drawn
on a state account. Also, the staff has been receiving all
the benefits that normally accrue to full-time state employees
working in the Lieutenant Governor's Office. These facts
indicate that the staff is inextricably tied to the Lieu-
tenant Governor's Office and, therefore, to the state.

        California case law provides a well-established general
rule for determining when an employer-employee relationship
is present:

                The right to control and direct the activities
                of the alleged employee or the manner and method
                in which the work is performed whether exercised
                or not, gives rise to the employment relationship.
                [Miller v. Long Beach Oil Dev. Co., 167 Cal.App.2d
                546, 550 (1959); Stevens v. Roman Catholic Bishop
                of Fresno, 49 Cal.App.3d 877 (1975).]


        The Office of the Lieutenant Governor exercises budgetary
control over the Study through the fiscal officer for the Study,
Richard Howe. The Lieutenant Governor has ultimate control over
the Study not only through the budgetary process but through his
ability to hire and fire as well. The Study's director is
appointed by the Lieutenant Governor, and is subject to dis-
missal by him. Because of the Lieutenant Governor's control
over the directorship, he also has control over the composition
of the staff. It is the courts' view that this ability to
control suggests that an employment relationship is present. 3/


_____________________________

        3/ From time to time portions of the Study have been sent
to the Lieutenant Governor's Office for comment and review.

Betty Jo Smith
December 5, 1977                                                  Page Four


                                    II.

     Assuming that the staff of the Study are state employees,
are they subject to the Political Reform Act? Section 87300
of the Government Code provides: "Every agency shall adopt
and promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to the
provisions of this article...". 4/

     As stated above, the Lieutenant Governor's Office organ-
ized and, in part controls, the implementation of the Study.
In light of this association with the Lieutenant Governor's
Office, the staff of the Study is either part of an agency
(Lieutenant Governor's Office) or an agency in and of itself.

     Thus, it would appear that the staff of the Study is
required to promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant
to Section 87300. Although you are not seeking an exemption
from Section 87300 pursuant to the procedures set forth in
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18751, we think it appropriate to
consider the provisions of that regulation in determining
whether to notice your agency for the adoption of a Conflict
of Interest Code.

     The Fair Political Practices Commission has provided by
regulation that an agency is entitled to an exemption from
the requirements of Section 87300 if (1) the agency will soon
be inoperable, or (2) the agency has no designated employees
within the meaning of Government Code Sections 82019 and
87302(a). 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18751(b).

     Although the staff need only satisfy one of the tests
set forth above in order to apply for an exemption, it satis-
fies both. The facts indicate that this Study will be com-
pleted in December. There exists no possibility that the
life of the Study will be extended beyond this date. On
this basis alone, the staff could apply for and be granted
an exemption from the requirements of Section 87300.

     Section 82019 excludes employees who possess no decision-
making authority from the definition of designated employee.
The Commission has formulated a standard for determining when
decision-making authority is present. The staff has decision-
making authority if it is capable of:


_____________________

4/ All references herein are to the Government Code
unless otherwise provided.

Betty Jo Smith
December 5, 1977                                                Page Five


     (a)  making a final governmental decision;

     (b)  compelling or preventing a governmental
decision from being made by virtue of its power
to initiate or veto the decision;

     (c)  making recommendations with are, and
over an extended period of time have been reg-
ularly approved without significant amendment
or modification by another governmental official
or agency.

                    2 Cal.Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(1)

     On the basis of the facts set forth above, it is our
understanding that the staff possesses no decision-making
authority of the type described in the regulation. There-
fore, the staff has no designated employees, as defined in
Section 82019, and accordingly would be entitled, on that
ground, to an exemption from the provisions of Section 87300.

     Since the Study will terminate prior to the adoption of
a Code, we have decided not to notice the staff to adopt a
Conflict of Interest Code. It should be pointed out that
although the staff of the Study need not draw a Code, the
staff is still subject to the general disqualification pro-
visions of the Political Reform Act if they become involved
in governmental decision-making.

     Section 87100 provides:

          No public official at any level of state
     or local government shall make, partici-
     pate in making or in any way attempt to
     use his official position to influence a
     governmental decision in which he knows
     or has reason to know he has a financial
     interest.

     It is important to note that Section 87100 applies to all
public officials regardless of whether they are subject to a
Conflict of Interest Code. Thus, if members of the staff make
or participate in the making of governmental decisions, Section
87100 is applicable even though we will not require the staff
to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code.

Betty Jo Smith
December 5, 1977                                                    Page Six


     I hope this information has been helpful.  If you have
any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact
this office.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Dwight Dickerson
                                        Conflicts of Interest
                                        Division


DD:gb

sections.question: The questions presented are: (1) Whether the above-described staff of the Study are federal or state employees, and (2) Given that the Study is a federally and state funded project administered by a state office, what provisions of Political Reform Act are applicable to the Study's staff?
sections.conclusion: It is our opinion that the staff (including the consultants) of this study are state employees. Although the Study receives a substantial amount of federal money, part of which pays salaries, the structure of the program suggests that the staff shall be considered state employees. Since the Study will terminate prior to the adoption of a Code, we have decided not to notice the staff to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code. It should be pointed out that although the staff of the Study need not draft a Code, the staff is still subject to the general disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act if they become involved in governmental decision-making.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether staff of a federally and state-funded Border Area Development Study administered by the Lieutenant Governor's Office are federal or state employees, and what Political Reform Act provisions apply to them.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded the Study staff are state employees and entitled to exemption from Conflict of Interest Code requirements based on the Study's imminent termination and staff's lack of decision-making authority, though they remain subject to general disqualification provisions under Section 87100 if involved in governmental decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document has clear Q&A structure. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not impact extraction. Footnotes and case citations included. Letter dated December 5, 1977 from Dwight Dickerson, FPPC Conflicts of Interest Division, to Ms. Betty Jo Smith.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The questions presented are: (1) Whether the above-described staff of the Study are federal or state employees, and (2) Given that the Study is a federally and state funded project administered by a state office, what provisions of Political Reform Act are applicable to the Study's staff?

CONCLUSION: It is our opinion that the staff (including the consultants) of this study are state employees. Although the Study receives a substantial amount of federal money, part of which pays salaries, the structure of the program suggests that the staff shall be considered state employees. Since the Study will terminate prior to the adoption of a Code, we have decided not to notice the staff to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code. It should be pointed out that although the staff of the Study need not draft a Code, the staff is still subject to the general disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act if they become involved in governmental decision-making.

embedding.summary: This December 5, 1977 FPPC advice letter addresses whether staff of a federally and state-funded Border Area Development Study are federal or state employees and what Political Reform Act provisions apply. The Commission concluded the staff are state employees entitled to exemption from Conflict of Interest Code requirements but remain subject to general disqualification provisions.
